questions.question: Which type of average is calculated by adding all values together and dividing by the number of values?
questions.explanation:
The mean is obtained by adding all the numbers in a data set and dividing the result by however many numbers there are.

**Common mistake:** Students often confuse the mean with the median, which is the middle value in an ordered list.

questions.question: Find the median of the following data set: 3, 3, 4, 7, 8, 10, 12.
questions.explanation:
The median is the number in the middle when values are arranged in ascending order. In this set of 7 items, the 4th item is 7.

**Common mistake:** A student might pick the middle number without ordering the list first.

questions.question: What is the mode of this data set: 5, 4, 10, 3, 3, 4, 7, 4, 8, 5?
questions.explanation:
The mode is the number which occurs most often. In this set, 4 appears three times, more than any other number.

**Common mistake:** Students may confuse the mode with the mean (5.3) or the median (4.5).

questions.question: Calculate the range of the following heights: 140 cm, 155 cm, 162 cm, 135 cm, 170 cm.
questions.explanation:
The range is found by subtracting the lowest value from the highest value: $170 - 135 = 35$.

**Common mistake:** Students sometimes provide the highest value as the range instead of the difference.

questions.question: Which measure of average is best to use when a data set contains extreme values (outliers)?
questions.explanation:
The median works well when there are extreme values because it is not affected by them, unlike the mean.

**Common mistake:** The mean is the most common average, so students often default to it without considering outliers.
